title: The gender pay gap among young academics
abstract: Abstract This research article investigates the impact of childbearing and horizontal segregation on income disparities among young academics in Hungary. Using a comprehensive, large-scale survey data set on the demographics and working conditions of academics in Hungary, this research highlights the precarious conditions in the Hungarian academic sector. The findings reveal that researchers and lecturers often rely on multiple sources of income and work extra hours to secure their livelihoods, creating a disadvantageous situation for women with young children, especially during the highly competitive early stages of their careers. The results suggest that the income differences between men and women are to a large extent explained by the so-called fatherhood wage premium and that horizontal gender segregation of the scientific fields has a significant effect on income disparities as well.
summary_en: Young Hungarian academics face substantial income inequality by gender — and this paper pinpoints the drivers. Using a large survey of Hungarian academics, the authors show the gap is explained primarily by the "fatherhood wage premium" (fathers earn more than non-fathers, while mothers don't earn less than non-mothers) and by horizontal gender segregation across scientific fields (women are overrepresented in lower-paid disciplines). The Hungarian academic sector is precarious: many researchers rely on multiple income sources, a situation especially disadvantageous for mothers of young children during early-career years.
data_used: Large-scale survey of Hungarian academics covering demographics, working conditions, and multiple income sources; cross-section of the Hungarian scientific workforce.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian higher-education and research-funding policy (NKFIH, Ministry for Culture and Innovation, university rectors). Concrete levers: family-friendly early-career funding schemes, evaluation criteria that discount parental leaves, and targeted support for scientific fields where women are overrepresented. The finding that the gap comes from fatherhood premium and field segregation means generic equal-pay rules miss the main drivers.
